getconf -a:
NAME
       getconf - retrieve configuration values from the database

SYNOPSIS
       getconf [-h] [-v] [-n] [-m] [-M] [-V] [-C] [-c] [-d] [-p] [-P] [-s] [-S] [-t] [-T] [-w] [-W] [-x] [-X] [-y] [-Y] [-z] [-Z] name...

DESCRIPTION
       getconf is used to retrieve configuration values from the database.  The database contains system and user configurable parameters.

OPTIONS
       -h, --help
              print this help message

       -v, --version
              print version information

       -n, --numeric
              numeric output only

       -m, --machine
              machine dependent configuration values

       -M, --machine-file
              read configuration from file specified by MACHINE_FILE environment variable

       -V, --vendor
              vendor specific configuration values

       -C, --category
              category of configuration values

       -c, --canonical-path
              canonical path for a given name

       -d, --define
              define a new configuration value

       -p, --path
              print the path to the database file

       -P, --prefix
              prefix for configuration names

       -s, --system
              system wide configuration values

       -S, --system-file
              read configuration from file specified by SYSTEM_FILE environment variable

       -t, --terminal
              terminal dependent configuration values

       -T, --terminal-file
              read configuration from file specified by TERMINAL_FILE environment variable

       -w, --window
              window system dependent configuration values

       -W, --window-file
              read configuration from file specified by WINDOW_FILE environment variable

       -x, --x-display
              X display dependent configuration values

       -X, --x-display-file
              read configuration from file specified by X_DISPLAY_FILE environment variable

       -y, --user
              user specific configuration values

       -Y, --user-file
              read configuration from file specified by USER_FILE environment variable

       -z, --zoneinfo
              zoneinfo dependent configuration values

       -Z, --zoneinfo-file
              read configuration from file specified by ZONEINFO_FILE environment variable

       name...
              the names of the configuration values to retrieve

EXIT STATUS
       getconf returns 0 if all requested configuration values were successfully retrieved.

       If an error occurs while retrieving a value, getconf prints an error message and continues with the next value.  The exit status is non-zero only if no values were specified or if an error occurred while reading from a file.

       If the -n option is used, getconf returns 0 if all requested configuration values have numeric output; otherwise it returns 1.

EXAMPLES
       getconf PATH_SEPARATOR

       getconf LONGEST_LINE

       getconf PWD_MAX

SEE ALSO
       confstr(3), sysconf(3)

BUGS
       None known.

cat /etc/init.d/cron:
#!/bin/sh

### BEGIN INIT INFO
# Provides:          cron
# Required-Start:    $local_fs $network
# Should-Start:      nscd
# Default-Start:     2 3 4 5
# Default-Stop:      0 1 6
# Short-Description:   Periodically run commands
### END INIT INFO

PATH=/sbin:/usr/sbin:/bin:/usr/bin
DAEMON=/usr/sbin/cron
NAME=cron
DESC="Periodically run commands"

test -x $DAEMON || exit 0

case "$1" in
  start)
    # Only start cron if the system clock is set.
    if [ -r /etc/adjtime ]; then
      echo "Starting $DESC database daemon: $NAME"
      start-stop-daemon --start --quiet --pidfile /var/run/cron.pid \
        --exec $DAEMON -- $@ || true
      ;;
  stop)
    echo "Stopping $DESC database daemon: $NAME"
    start-stop-daemon --stop --quiet --oknodo --pidfile /var/run/cron.pid \
      --exec $DAEMON -- $@ || true
    ;;
  restart|reload)
    $0 stop
    $0 start
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Usage: $0 {start|stop|restart|reload}" >&2
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

exit 0

shutdown --help:
Usage: shutdown [OPTION]... TIME[DATE] ACTION

  -h, --halt           halt the system
  -p, --poweroff       power off the system
  -r, --reboot         reboot the system
  -P, --powerdown      power down the system (same as --poweroff)
  -c, --cancel         cancel a pending shutdown
  -k, --kill           send a SIGTERM to all processes
  -a, --askdisk       ask for confirmation before shutting down due to low disk space
  -A, --asknetwork     ask for confirmation before shutting down due to network problems
  -q, --quiet          suppress status messages
  -n, --no-wall        do not send wall message
  -s, --shutdown      shutdown the system (default)
  -H, --no-halt       do not halt the system after shutdown
  -P, --no-poweroff   do not power off the system after shutdown

TIME[DATE] is in the format 'HHMM' or 'HH:MM' for a time of day, or 'NOW' to shut down immediately.

ACTION is one of:
  now          shut down immediately
  +m           shut down in m minutes
  @yearmonthdatehourminute     shut down at year-month-date hour:minute
  nextday       shut down at the same time tomorrow
  weekday      shut down at the same time on the next weekday (i.e. not a weekend)
  reboot       shut down and then reboot
  halt         shut down and then halt

Examples:
  shutdown now           shut down immediately
  shutdown +10          shut down in 10 minutes
  shutdown 12:00        shut down at noon
  shutdown @year1234:05:30  shut down on year 1234 at 05:30
  shutdown nextday       shut down tomorrow at the same time
ps --help simple:
Usage: ps [options]

Options:
  -A, --all            show all processes
  -a, --aux            show information about processes owned by you and other users
  -c, --columns        specify which output columns to display (see below)
  -e, --forest         show process hierarchy in a tree format
  -f, --full           wide output; show full format for each process
  -h, --help            print this help message and exit
  -l, --long           long format; show detailed information about each process
  -L, --linelimit      set the maximum number of lines to display (default is unlimited)
  -n, --deselect       select processes NOT matching specified criteria
  -o, --output         specify which output columns to display (see below)
  -p, --pid            show information for the process with the ID <pid>
  -q, --quiet          suppress all warnings and diagnostic messages
  -r, --running        show only running processes
  -s, --stat           show information about each process in a compact format
  -t, --tty            show processes attached to specified terminal
  -u, --user           show processes owned by user <user>
  -V, --version        print version and exit
  -v, --verbose        increase verbosity level (can be used multiple times)
  -x, --forensic       show detailed information about each process

Columns:
  pid         process ID
  ppid        parent process ID
  uid         real user ID
  euid        effective user ID
  suid        saved set-user-ID mode
  fsuid       filesystem-locked user ID
  gid         real group ID
  egid        effective group ID
  sgid        saved set-group-ID mode
  fsgid       filesystem-locked group ID
  pid, ppid   process and parent process IDs (same as -o pid,ppid)
  stat        process status (running or zombie)
  start       start time of the process
  vsize       virtual memory size in bytes
  rss         resident set size in bytes
  wchan       current system call being executed by the process

Output format:
  ps can be used to display information about processes in a variety of formats.
  The -o option is used to specify which columns are displayed.
